scores.name: hardship_handling
scores.reason: Agent 1 correctly treated the borrower's opening statement as hardship, verified identity first, then asked a single hardship timing question and recorded unemployment/no income. Agent 2 correctly recognized that with unemployment and no ability to commit, hardship review was the only live path and did not push payment. However, the system used a 21-day standard instead of the policy-required 30-day standard, which is a material policy mismatch affecting hardship routing.

scores.name: policy_safe_resolution
scores.reason: The system avoided false threats, unauthorized discounts, and improper pressure, and Agent 2 stayed in role by not negotiating when hardship review was the only live path. But the lender/company policy provided is Nira, while the agents repeatedly referenced Slice policy, creating a cross-policy mismatch. More importantly, both assessment and resolution applied an unsupported 21-day threshold instead of the required 30-day rule, so the chosen next step was justified using the wrong policy frame. Agent 3 also failed to state a supported next step such as hardship review pending, credit reporting review, or legal review; it merely said nothing further is needed.

scores.name: required_disclosures_and_identity
scores.reason: Agent 1 clearly disclosed AI identity and logging at the start and used a masked account ending for privacy-safe identification. Identity verification used partial information. However, the company name/policy context was inconsistent: the transcript says the lender policy is Nira, but the agent identified itself as acting on behalf of Slice. That inconsistency weakens compliance confidence and could misidentify the principal. Later stages appropriately relied on prior disclosures rather than re-verifying.

scores.name: stop_contact_and_non_harassment
scores.reason: No harassment occurred. The borrower did not explicitly request full stop-contact; instead they requested limited contact only if an approved hardship option becomes available. Agent 2 acknowledged and recorded that preference, and later stages honored it. Professional tone was maintained throughout.

scores.name: handoff_execution
scores.reason: Continuity was generally strong. Agent 1 performed assessment first: it established account context, verified identity appropriately with partial identifiers, recognized hardship, and gathered the borrower's financial situation before handoff. Agent 1 did not improperly negotiate or threaten. Agent 2 stayed in the resolution/hardship-routing role and did not repeat verification or debt establishment. Agent 3 also avoided redundant questioning. The main seam issue is modality/continuity inconsistency: Agent 2 was supposed to be voice but the transcript remained chat-like, and all stages carried forward the same incorrect 21-day standard rather than the required 30-day policy.
